title: The square knot
subtitle: The core knot of macramé: alternating half knots to build flat or twisted cord
excerpt: The square knot is built from two half knots tied in opposite directions. A single square knot is flat and symmetrical. Repeating the same half knot without alternating produces a spiral twist. Alternating square knots over groups of four cords creates the open lattice patterns central to macramé.
sourceNotes: The square knot is a standard knot in macramé and textile knotting. Its structure is equivalent to the reef knot in rope work. Observation-led.

content.text: uses four cords: two outer working cords and two central
. The working cords cross over and under the core cords alternately, each crossing tying one
. Two half knots (one from the left, one from the right) complete one square knot.

steps: Hold four cords: working cord left (A), core cord left-centre (B), core cord right-centre (C), working cord right (D).
First half knot: bring A over B and C, then lay it to the right.
Bring D under B and C, then up through the loop that A has formed on the right side.
Pull A and D outward to tighten. You now have the first half knot.
Second half knot: bring D over B and C, then lay it to the left.
Bring A under B and C, then up through the loop that D has formed on the left side.
Pull A and D outward to tighten. One square knot is complete.

content.text: Spiral twist from repeated half knots

Repeating only the first half knot (always crossing A over C) produces a spiral that rotates with every two or three knots. This is called a half hitch twist or half knot spiral, and is often used for decorative cords in plant hanger necks.

lattice: tie square knots across the full width in row 1, using cords 1-4, 5-8, 9-12, and so on. In row 2, skip the first two cords and tie square knots using cords 3-6, 7-10, 11-14. In row 3, return to cords 1-4. The offset creates the diamond lattice pattern used in wall hangings and bags.

items.fix: After each half knot, pull both working cords straight outward (at 90 degrees to the core cords) and tighten evenly. Keep your working hand position consistent.
items.cause: The working cords were pulled at different angles or with different force on each half knot.
items.symptom: Knots are uneven: some tight, some loose

items.fix: Ensure the second half knot starts from the opposite side to the first. In a square knot, the first cross comes from the left and the second from the right (or vice versa).
items.cause: The second half knot is being tied in the same direction as the first, producing a spiral.
items.symptom: Square knots are twisting rather than lying flat
